sections.heading: Seruvakula demands smarter attacking play
sections.body: Interim coach Senirusi Seruvakula has challenged the Flying Fijians to improve their discipline and decision-making for today's clash. While sticking to an attacking brand of rugby, Seruvakula noted that his side must improve its discipline and decision-making following the opening loss to Wales. Out wide, Jiuta Wainiqolo enters the match in scorching form, identified by coverage as the most significant individual threat to the English defensive line.

sections.heading: Clean bill of health for Liverpool
sections.body: No fresh injury news in today's coverage has emerged from the Fiji camp as they prepare for the heat of Liverpool. This silence suggests the interim coaching staff have been able to proceed with their prepared tactical plan without late disruptions to the matchday 23. Maintaining a stable roster is a boost for a side looking to improve cohesion following their 15-point loss in Round 1.

sections.heading: Borthwick turns to youth and Slade
sections.body: Steve Borthwick has recalled Henry Slade to the starting lineup as England attempts to snap a five-match losing streak. The hosts have named three uncapped players on the bench, including teenage talent Noah Caluori. Borthwick expects a difficult encounter on Merseyside, noting that Fiji "are a dangerous side who will demand the very best from us" as he seeks to avoid another defeat.

sections.heading: Pressure to climb the log
sections.body: Fiji goes into the weekend in the lower reaches of the table and requires a victory to move toward the middle of the pack. After the opening loss to Wales, securing points in Liverpool is vital before the championship continues in Round 3. A win today would capitalize on England’s recent instability and prevent the Flying Fijians from falling adrift in the early tournament stages.

sections.heading: Slade returns as Borthwick eyes debutants
sections.body: Steve Borthwick has recalled Henry Slade to the starting XV for today’s encounter, with Tommy Freeman moving to the wing. Guy Pepper also joins the side for the match in Liverpool. Looking to inject fresh energy, the coach has named three uncapped players on the bench: George Kloska, Benhard Janse van Rensburg, and teenager Noah Caluori, who are all set for their first international appearances.

sections.heading: Squad status remains consistent
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today’s coverage following the team announcement earlier this week. Borthwick has settled on his match-day 23, and no further updates regarding player availability have been reported by the team or media sources. The squad remains as selected on Thursday, with Jamie George continuing to lead the side in the absence of the rested Maro Itoje.

sections.heading: Seruvakula promises attacking intent
sections.body: Fiji interim coach Senirusi Seruvakula has confirmed his side will maintain their trademark attacking brand of rugby today. However, he admitted the team must improve their discipline and decision-making following their opening defeat to Wales. Fiji will be looking to build on their historic 2023 victory over England, with Seruvakula urging his players to be smarter in their execution throughout the 80 minutes.

sections.heading: Pressure mounts as winless run looms
sections.body: Following the opening defeat to South Africa, England are searching for their first win of the campaign. Borthwick is under significant pressure to avoid a sixth successive Test defeat, a streak that would mirror the team's struggles from 2006. With the tour of Argentina approaching next, today’s result is viewed as a vital opportunity to relieve tension and shift the current narrative.

sections.heading: Jalibert starts despite late fitness test
sections.body: France has confirmed a lineup for today's match featuring an experienced spine, including Peato Mauvaka, Emmanuel Meafou, and Romain Ntamack. Matthieu Jalibert has been selected to start at fullback, though he faces a late fitness test on a troublesome calf. Coach Fabien Galthie has maintained faith in his primary playmakers as the team looks to bounce back from last week's narrow defeat.

sections.heading: Penaud remains sidelined for tournament
sections.body: Damian Penaud is unavailable for today's Test and the remainder of the Nations Championship matches this month. The winger sustained a calf injury during the opening loss to New Zealand. There has been no fresh injury news regarding other squad members in today's coverage, suggesting the rest of the starting group remains available for selection at Suncorp Stadium.

sections.heading: Wallabies blood rookie flyhalf in Brisbane
sections.body: Australia has turned to rookie Declan Meredith at flyhalf following an injury crisis in their ranks. The Wallabies are also missing Carter Gordon and Tom Hooper for this clash. Despite these personnel struggles, Fabien Galthie has warned that the hosts remain a significant threat, noting that Australia might "blow hot and cold" while both teams remain desperate to recover from opening-round defeats.

sections.heading: Finding momentum in the middle of the pack
sections.body: France currently sits in the middle of the log after an opening defeat, having secured zero competition points. With both teams suffering two-point losses in the first round, today’s result is pivotal for establishing momentum in the tournament. A victory in Brisbane would provide a necessary reset for the side before they continue their campaign, while another loss would leave them at 0-2.

sections.heading: Farrell shuffles deck for Japanese challenge
sections.body: Andy Farrell has rotated his side significantly for today's match, making nine changes to the starting XV that edged Australia. Tadhg Beirne will captain the team for the first time, while Sean Jansen earns his first cap in the back row. Ciarán Frawley is set for his first start at out-half, guiding a new-look lineup that includes Ronan Kelleher earning his 50th cap.

sections.heading: Squad health update
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage regarding the traveling squad. Robert Baloucoune remains sidelined after his late withdrawal from the opening Test against Australia, and he is not included in the matchday 23 for this encounter. All other players in the 36-man touring group have been factored into the selection plans for this week's rotation.

sections.heading: Eddie Jones returns to the coaching box
sections.body: Japan coach Eddie Jones is back from his four-match ban to lead a side that features one change, with Taira Main replacing Kippei Ishida on the wing. Jones has suggested he expects Japan to dominate the Irish scrum. The Brave Blossoms arrive following a 27-10 victory over Italy, having restricted the Azzurri to a single try in a disciplined opening round performance.

sections.heading: Looking to build momentum
sections.body: Ireland enters today's match sitting fifth in the 12-team tournament table following a narrow 33-31 victory against Australia. Securing a win today is crucial for Ireland to maintain their unbeaten start to the Nations Championship before the squad travels to Auckland for next weekend’s fixture against the All Blacks. Consistency remains the primary objective for this rotated side in Newcastle.

sections.heading: Allan and Lynagh return to starting backline
sections.body: Italy have made three changes to the starting lineup following the opening defeat to Japan. Fullback Tommaso Allan and wing Louis Lynagh have been recalled to the XV to inject fresh energy into the attack. Captain Michele Lamaro continues to lead from the flank in a side featuring an otherwise settled core as they prepare for the immense challenge of facing the All Blacks in Wellington.

sections.heading: Squad status remains stable before kickoff
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage following the announcement of the starting XV. The coaching staff has maintained consistency in their selections, and no players have been ruled out of Saturday's Test since the team was named earlier this week. The group is set to take the field with the personnel confirmed in Thursday's final match-day squad update.

sections.heading: All Blacks name powerful side for Wellington Test
sections.body: New Zealand have confirmed a formidable lineup for their home fixture, with Codie Taylor captaining the side from hooker and Ardie Savea starting at No. 8. Notably, Anton Segner is set to become the first Germany-born player to represent the All Blacks should he appear from the bench. New Zealand enter this clash after a narrow victory over France in their tournament opener last weekend.

sections.heading: Italy seek momentum after difficult tournament start
sections.body: Following the opening round loss to Japan, Italy find themselves needing a significantly improved performance to climb the log. A result against a New Zealand side that has won each of the last four meetings between these nations would be a massive statement. Every point remains critical ahead of the upcoming Finals Weekend as Italy look to register their first win of this championship.

sections.heading: Finn Russell returns to start in Pretoria
sections.body: Finn Russell is back in the Scotland starting lineup for today’s fixture against South Africa at Loftus Versfeld. The fly-half returns following his recovery from a calf injury. Gregor Townsend has made 3 changes in total to the side that secured a win over Argentina last weekend, as Scotland look to build on their current position in the Nations Championship.

sections.heading: Fitness update regarding the touring squad
sections.body: Today’s coverage confirms that Finn Russell has recovered from the calf injury that sidelined him for the opening round of the tournament. Regarding other members of the squad, there is no further information or fresh injury news provided in today’s reports concerning the availability of players for this afternoon’s Test match at Loftus Versfeld.

sections.heading: Springboks name side for Loftus Versfeld clash
sections.body: South Africa have named Boan Venter, Johan Grobbelaar, and Wilco Louw in the front row for today. The hosts feature Handré Pollard at fly-half and have made 10 changes to their starting side following their win over England. Assistant coach Mzwandile Stick rejected the suggestion that this selection constitutes a secondary team, citing the depth of the world champions.

sections.heading: Points available at Loftus Versfeld
sections.body: Scotland arrive at this second round of the tournament after an opening victory in Argentina. With the Scots ranked fifth in the world, they face a South African side currently at the top of the global rankings. Both teams are looking to secure vital tournament points to strengthen their respective standing within the 2026 Nations Championship table.

sections.heading: Steady as she goes
sections.body: Wales opened the Nations Championship with intent, banking a 39-24 win over Fiji, and the build-up to Round 2 has carried the same tone. Nothing here smells of wholesale change — a group that clicked early gets the chance to show it wasn't a one-off, with Argentina up next to test whether that form travels on the road.

sections.heading: Clean bill through Thursday
sections.body: Nothing new has emerged out of camp since Thursday, and the squad that started against Fiji remains available heading into Saturday's Test in Argentina. No knocks, no late scares, no fitness question marks — about as clean a build-up as Wales could hope for going into a Round 2 away trip.

sections.heading: Pumas bite hardest at home
sections.body: Argentina go into Round 2 on home soil, and history says the Pumas are hardest to shift there — physical up front, patient with ball in hand, happy to grind a game down. Expect their forward pack to dominate the selection talk this week, with that battle shaping as the real test of Wales's opening-round form.

sections.heading: Second of 12, for now
sections.body: One round in, Wales sit second of 12, unbeaten, with a scoring difference of plus 15 after the Fiji win. Saturday's trip to face Argentina is the first proper gauge of where this squad stands away from home — win it, and second place becomes very hard for anyone else to argue with this weekend.

sections.heading: Five changes for Argentina
sections.body: Argentina has announced an experienced lineup featuring 5 changes to the starting XV for the Round 2 fixture. Notably, lock Guido Petti is set to reach 100 caps for his country. The side looks to rebound in San Juan following the 47-38 loss to Scotland in the opening round of the tournament.

sections.heading: No fresh updates
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage regarding the Argentina squad. The team has completed its preparations for the upcoming match in San Juan, and the selection reflects the group named earlier in the week to face the visitors.

sections.heading: Wales make 3 changes
sections.body: Wales head coach Steve Tandy has made 3 changes to his starting XV. Louis Rees-Zammit moves to the bench for this fixture. The visitors arrive in San Juan following a 39-24 victory over Fiji and will look to extend their current winning form when they take to the field at Estadio San Juan del Bicentenario.

sections.heading: Seeking a first win
sections.body: Both teams enter this second round encounter seeking their first victory of the Nations Championship. Following a 38-47 result in their opener, Argentina currently sits 9th on the log. A win today is essential for the Pumas to climb the standings as they look to improve upon their performance from the opening weekend.

sections.heading: Declan Meredith debuts at flyhalf
sections.body: Declan Meredith has been selected to make his test debut for the Wallabies at flyhalf in today’s match at Suncorp Stadium. Captain Harry Wilson leads the side from the back of the scrum. Joseph-Aukuso Suaalii joins Len Ikitau in the midfield, while the starting pack features Angus Bell, Josh Nasser, and Allan Alaalatoa in the front row.

sections.heading: Flyhalf options depleted
sections.body: No fresh injury news is contained in today's coverage. Earlier reporting confirmed that Carter Gordon and loose forward Tom Hooper were ruled out of this test due to injury. The team has turned to Declan Meredith to fill the flyhalf position following these roster changes during the week.

sections.heading: France names experienced spine
sections.body: France head coach Fabien Galthie has selected an experienced core for today's match, including Peato Mauvaka, Emmanuel Meafou, Romain Ntamack, Maxime Lucu, and Matthieu Jalibert. Jalibert is named at fullback, though he faced a fitness test on a calf injury. Damian Penaud remains unavailable for the remainder of the Nations Championship after sustaining a calf injury last week.

sections.heading: Seeking first tournament win
sections.body: Following a 31-33 loss to Ireland in the opening round, Australia currently sits 8th in the tournament standings. France also arrives after a narrow defeat to New Zealand. Both teams are looking to secure their first victory of the Nations Championship to avoid an 0-2 start to their campaign this morning.

sections.heading: Eddie Jones returns to lead side
sections.body: Eddie Jones is back in the coach's box for Japan after serving his four-match ban. The side shows only one change from the victory over Italy, with Taira Main entering the starting lineup on the wing. Jones has wasted no time in restarting his customary mind games, stating: "We expect to dominate the Irish scrum."

sections.heading: Squad status remains stable
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage regarding the Japan camp. The coaching staff has maintained continuity in their selection, with only one alteration to the starting XV following the opening round. By contrast, the opposition arrives with several players unavailable for selection, including the hamstrung Robert Baloucoune, who was a late withdrawal from their previous fixture.

sections.heading: Farrell rotates his squad
sections.body: Ireland head coach Andy Farrell has made nine changes to his starting side for this encounter in Newcastle. Tadhg Beirne captains the team for the first time as the tourists look to build on their opening win against Australia. The selection includes four uncapped players in the matchday 23, with Ciarán Frawley handed his first start at out-half alongside scrum-half Craig Casey.

sections.heading: Japan eyes second win
sections.body: Following a successful opening weekend against Italy, Japan continues its Nations Championship campaign in what is technically an away game for the Brave Blossoms. With Ireland aiming to remain unbeaten, today’s result is critical for Japan’s momentum in the tournament. A strong performance here remains the primary focus before the squad prepares to host France in Tokyo.

sections.heading: All Blacks name side with new cap included
sections.body: The All Blacks have named their starting side to face Italy in Wellington. Ardie Savea will start at No. 8, with Codie Taylor captaining the team from hooker. Anton Segner is included among the reserves and is in line to become the first Germany-born player to earn a cap for the All Blacks.

sections.heading: No fresh updates from the camp
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage regarding the All Blacks squad. Scott Barrett remains unavailable for the team due to a back injury that was previously noted when the wider squad was first selected for this series.

sections.heading: Italy makes three changes to their starting lineup
sections.body: Italy has confirmed three changes to their starting side following their opening defeat to Japan. Head coach Gonzalo Quesada has recalled fullback Tommaso Allan and wing Louis Lynagh into the starting XV. The side is led by captain Michele Lamaro, who starts at flanker for the Azzurri as they seek their first win of the tournament.

sections.heading: All Blacks seek points in second round
sections.body: New Zealand enters this round holding four competition points after their opening win against France. Sitting second in the Southern Hemisphere table, the team is looking to build on their performance in the inaugural Nations Championship. A win in this match is essential for New Zealand to maintain their position within the conference standings before heading to the northern hemisphere.

sections.heading: Erasmus shuffles pack for Scotland
sections.body: The Springboks have opted for a rotation-heavy approach for this afternoon’s second-round fixture. Rassie Erasmus has made changes to the lineup that dispatched England, aiming to test fresh combinations. The starting XV features Boan Venter, Johan Grobbelaar, and Wilco Louw in the front row, with Embrose Papier starting at scrum-half and Handré Pollard continuing at fly-half as the team looks to build on their opening victory.

sections.heading: Squad status remains stable
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage regarding the South African squad. The lineup finalized for today’s match appears to be the group currently available to the coaching staff, as reports have focused primarily on the tactical rotations and the selection changes made by the Springbok management team heading into this afternoon’s contest at Loftus Versfeld.

sections.heading: Russell returns as Scots eye upset
sections.body: Scotland have named star fly-half Finn Russell in their starting XV, a move captain Sione Tuipulotu believes will significantly alter their tactical approach. Gregor Townsend, who has described the Springboks as “the biggest challenge in world rugby,” leads a side coming off a 47-38 win over Argentina. The visitors are looking to reverse a run of three consecutive defeats against the world champions.

sections.heading: Maintaining momentum at home
sections.body: Following a successful opening round against England, the Springboks seek to consolidate their position within the Nations Championship. Success today would keep South Africa at the front of the pack, while Scotland aim to build on their own strong start to the tournament. With the home crowd behind them, the Boks are looking to secure a vital win to maintain their current ranking.
